request.question: Londonderry Borough Police are the force in the county that is part of what country?

title: Reading Borough Police
Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920
St Ives Borough Police
County Londonderry
Penzance Borough Police
Law enforcement in Ireland
Newcastle upon Tyne City Police
Londonderry Borough Police
Folkestone Borough Police
Ferguson baronets
sentences: Reading Borough Police was a police force for the borough of Reading in the United Kingdom.

The force was created on 21 February 1836, at which time it had a strength of 30 constables, two sergeants and two inspectors. Towards the end of the 19th century, Reading Borough Police had increased in size to 62 officers.
However, the local population had risen to around 60,500, which meant one officer for every 1,000 inhabitants.
By the time of the First World War the force had an establishment of 113 officers, however, due to military service only 30 officers were patrolling Reading.
An election to Londonderry County Borough Council took place in January 1920 as part of that year's Irish local elections.
The Local Government (Ireland) Act 1919 had seen elections for local government in Ireland change to a more proportional system.
As a result Unionists lost control over Londonderry borough council, which for the first time passed into the control of the city's Catholic majority.
St Ives Borough Police was the borough police force for the town of St Ives, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1889.
It was established following the passage of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835.
The force was unique in that it only boasted a single constable at any one time throughout its entire existence.
Since 1972, the counties in Northern Ireland; including Londonderry; have no longer been used by the state as part of the local administration.
Following further reforms in 2015, the area is now governed under three different districts; Derry and Strabane, Causeway Coast and Glens and Mid-Ulster.
Despite no longer being used for local government and administrative purposes, it is sometimes used in a cultural context in All-Ireland sporting and cultural events (ie - Derry GAA).
Penzance Borough Police was the police force for the borough and corporate town of Penzance, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1947.
It was formed following the passing of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835, which reformed all UK boroughs, and stipulated that each appoint a Watch Committee to oversee a police force.
The police force formed part of the commonality of the town’s government, led by an elected Mayor, six aldermen and eighteen councillors.
This is a description of law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.

Before the Republic (then called the Irish Free State) left the union, one police force - the Royal Irish Constabulary - policed almost the whole island (aside from Dublin, where the Dublin Metropolitan Police were the main force, Belfast where the Belfast Borough Police were the main force and the borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC.
See List of defunct law enforcement agencies in the United Kingdom and Irish Police records and also Royal Irish Constabulary Forum for other forces in Ireland that have been disbanded.)
Newcastle upon Tyne City Police also known as Newcastle upon Tyne Borough Police, was the Home office Police force and a Constabulary that served the City of Newcastle upon Tyne with origins in 1836.
On 1 April 1969 it was amalgamated into the Northumberland Constabulary under the Police Act 1964.
The first Modern Police Force was formed in Newcastle in 1836, 3 years before the County Police Act of 1839.
The Londonderry Borough Police was the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870, nicknamed the Horney Dicks after the bones used in their helmets.
They replaced the earlier town watch and were in turn replaced by the Royal Irish Constabulary.
The police force was established by the Londonderry Improvement Act 1848 and were governed by the Londonderry Corporation.
Its abolition was recommended by a commission of inquiry into sectarian riots in the city in 1869.

The commission felt the force, having the form of a town watch, was inadequate to policing serious crime; it also noted, but did not endorse, allegations that the police discriminated against Roman Catholics.
Folkestone Borough Police was the borough police force for the town of Folkestone in Kent.
It was formed in 1851 under the terms of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835.
The Ferguson Baronetcy, of the City of Londonderry, was a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom.
It was created on 7 October 1801 for Andrew Ferguson, who had previously represented Londonderry Borough in the Irish House of Commons.
The second Baronet represented Londonderry in the British House of Commons.
The title became extinct on his death in 1860.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Londonderry Borough Police are the force in the county that is part of what country?
hop1_docs: Londonderry Borough Police | The Londonderry Borough Police was the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870, nicknamed the Horney Dicks after the bones used in their helmets.  They replaced the earlier town watch and were in turn replaced by the Royal Irish Constabulary.  The police force was established by the Londonderry Improvement Act 1848 and were governed by the Londonderry Corporation.  Its abolition was recommended by a commission of inquiry into sectarian riots in the city in 1869.  The commission felt the force, having the form of a town watch, was inadequate to policing serious crime; it also noted, but did not endorse, allegations that the police discriminated against Roman Catholics.

Law enforcement in Ireland | This is a description of law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.  Before the Republic (then called the Irish Free State) left the union, one police force - the Royal Irish Constabulary - policed almost the whole island (aside from Dublin, where the Dublin Metropolitan Police were the main force, Belfast where the Belfast Borough Police were the main force and the borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC.  See List of defunct law enforcement agencies in the United Kingdom and Irish Police records and also Royal Irish Constabulary Forum for other forces in Ireland that have been disbanded.) .

Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920 | An election to Londonderry County Borough Council took place in January 1920 as part of that year's Irish local elections.  The Local Government (Ireland) Act 1919 had seen elections for local government in Ireland change to a more proportional system.  As a result Unionists lost control over Londonderry borough council, which for the first time passed into the control of the city's Catholic majority.

Barnsley Borough Police | Barnsley Borough Police was the police force operating in county borough of Barnsley, England until 1 October 1968.  The force was merged with other forces in 1968 to form the West Yorkshire Constabulary.  The area passed to the South Yorkshire Police in 1974.

Penzance Borough Police | Penzance Borough Police was the police force for the borough and corporate town of Penzance, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1947.  It was formed following the passing of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835, which reformed all UK boroughs, and stipulated that each appoint a Watch Committee to oversee a police force.  The police force formed part of the commonality of the town’s government, led by an elected Mayor, six aldermen and eighteen councillors.

Doncaster Borough Police | Doncaster Borough Police was the police force operating in the county borough of Doncaster, England until 1 October, 1968.  The force was then merged with others to form a West Yorkshire Constabulary.  From 1974 its area passed to the South Yorkshire Police.
Abingdon Borough Police | Abingdon Borough Police was the police force responsible for policing the Borough of Abingdon, situated in the county of Berkshire, England until 1889.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Londonderry Borough Police — "County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870" (Londonderry Borough Police [1]).
  - Contextual law‑enforcement placement — "law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland" and "borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force" (Law enforcement in Ireland [2]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Variants in the passages: "Londonderry Borough Police" (Londonderry Borough Police [1]); "borough of Londonderry" / "Londonderry County Borough" (Law enforcement in Ireland [2]; Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920 [3]).
  - The passages use the token "Ireland" and also mention "Northern Ireland" but do NOT unambiguously state which sovereign country County Londonderry currently belongs to.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "County Londonderry" (as named in passage [1]).
  - Missing fact needed: the sovereign country that County Londonderry is part of (exact current country name).
- Retrieval direction (priority order):
  1) Open the "County Londonderry" page — check lede/infobox and 'History'/'Administration' sections for "Country" or "Sovereign state".
     - Search keywords: "County Londonderry country", "County Londonderry infobox country".
  2) Open the "Londonderry" (Derry) page — check lede/infobox and 'Administration' section for county/country affiliation.
     - Search keywords: "Londonderry Derry county country".
  3) If needed, open "List of counties of Northern Ireland" and "Provinces and counties of Ireland" pages to confirm which sovereign state the county is listed under.
     - Search keywords: "counties of Northern Ireland list", "County Londonderry Northern Ireland".
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry | This is a list of people to have served as Lord Lieutenant of the city of Londonderry (originally formally "County of the City of Londonderry", later the "County Borough of Londonderry").
Londonderry, Guernsey County, Ohio | Londonderry is an unincorporated community in Londonderry Township, Guernsey County, Ohio, United States.
Custos Rotulorum of County Londonderry and Down | The Custos Rotulorum of Londonderry and Down was the highest civil officer in counties Londonderry and Down.
Marquess of Londonderry | Marquess of Londonderry, of the County of Londonderry ( ), is a title in the Peerage of Ireland.

McFarland baronets | The McFarland Baronetcy, of Aberfoyle in the County of Londonderry, is a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom.  It was created on 23 January 1914 for the Irish businessman John McFarland.  He was founder of and a partner in the firm of McCrea and McFarland, engineering contractors, of Belfast and Derry, Chairman of Mulhollands, drapers, and owner of the Lough Swilly Steamship Company, and also served as Mayor of Londonderry.  He was succeeded by his son, the second Baronet.  He was a businessman, a Senator of Northern Ireland, Mayor of Londonderry, Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry and an Irish Rugby union international.  The third Baronet has been a member of the Londonderry Corporation and a Deputy Lieutenant of the City of the County of Londonderry from 1956 to 1982.

Roe Valley Country Park | The Roe Valley Country Park is a forested area containing part of the River Roe, south west of Limavady, County Londonderry, Northern Ireland.  It is maintained by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, which is part of the Department of the Environment of Northern Ireland.
Londonderry Graving Dock railway station | Londonderry Graving Dock railway station served Derry in County Londonderry in Northern Ireland.
hop2_titles: Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry
Custos Rotulorum of County Londonderry and Down
response.summary_2: County Londonderry is in Northern Ireland. County Londonderry is shown in the passages as "County Londonderry, Northern Ireland."
